rationale_for_baseline_tier: Witnessed sudden collapse with sustained unresponsiveness, agonal (not normal) breathing, central cyanosis and flaccidity — this is cardiac arrest, an immediate threat to life measured in minutes. T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier, coupled with immediate bystander CPR and early AED use. There is no lower-tier option: survival is determined by seconds-to-compressions and time-to-first-shock.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unresponsive person who is not breathing normally (agonal gasps)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — within seconds; call 000 and start compressions immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: This is the definition of cardiac arrest for a lay rescuer and mandates immediate 000 plus chest compressions; every minute of delay costs roughly 10% of survival.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is an emergency — his heart may have stopped. Call 000 now (or tell me it's done) and put me on speaker. Start pushing hard and fast in the centre of his chest, about twice a second, and don't stop. I'll count with you.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe chest pain immediately before the collapse (ischaemic precipitant)
escalation_edges.rationale: Confirms a likely cardiac cause and is critical handover information for paramedics and hospital reperfusion, but does not alter the immediate action.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Tell the paramedics he clutched his chest in pain just before he collapsed — that helps them treat the cause.

escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: As soon as an AED can be fetched — do not interrupt compressions to look for one if alone
escalation_edges.rationale: Early defibrillation of a shockable rhythm is the highest-yield reversible intervention; the AED analyses the rhythm and advises the shock.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If there's a defibrillator nearby, or someone can bring one, turn it on and follow its voice — it will tell you where to put the pads and whether to shock. Stand clear when it says to, then keep doing compressions.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Return of spontaneous circulation (he starts breathing normally, moving, or waking)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately on any sign of life
escalation_edges.rationale: Even after ROSC the patient remains critically unwell and re-arrest is common; stop compressions, place on the side, keep warm, and stay with 000 for continued ambulance response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he starts breathing normally or moving, gently roll him onto his side, keep him warm, and stay on the line — the ambulance is still needed urgently.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The only person present is a distressed adult child in severe shock, performing CPR alone
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Use calm, simple, repetitive coaching; count compressions aloud with them; reassure that compression-only CPR is correct and that they cannot make things worse. If truly alone, prioritise calling 000 on speaker and continuous compressions over fetching an AED.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Single bystander — no second rescuer to divide tasks
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Have the bystander phone 000 on speaker and keep compressing rather than leaving the patient; only retrieve an AED if it is immediately to hand.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance immediately and say a man has collapsed, is unresponsive and not breathing normally. Put the phone on speaker. Start hands-only CPR now — push hard and fast in the centre of his chest, about twice a second, and don't stop. If a defibrillator (AED) is available, turn it on and follow its prompts. Keep going without interruption until the ambulance crew take over or he shows clear signs of life.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately and stay on the line
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a life-threatening emergency that must never wait for clinic hours or an after-hours service.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The receiving hospital will document the arrest, resuscitation and any cause found; the GP should follow up cardiac risk factors, secondary prevention, and family/bystander support and CPR training afterwards.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Anything short of immediate 000 + CPR coaching — e.g. treating as a faint/seizure to observe, advising a GP or ED self-presentation, or 'call back if he doesn't wake'.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delayed or absent compressions and defibrillation in a witnessed shockable arrest lead directly to anoxic brain death within minutes — an unrecoverable outcome.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising a serious emergency but routing to urgent care / ED transport instead of 000 with concurrent bystander CPR.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here — T5 is the ceiling and the correct tier; there is no higher tier to over-call to.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the occasional gasping means 'he is still breathing' (and therefore not in arrest).
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat agonal gasping in an unresponsive person as cardiac arrest and start CPR — err on the side of compressions; you cannot harm a person whose heart has stopped by starting CPR.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Withholding CPR because the patient is 'still gasping', or pausing to determine the rhythm/pulse before acting.
